title: Hungarian Language Basics
subtitle: An introduction to one of Europe's most unique languages

children.children: Hungarian (magyar) is spoken by ~13 million people and is one of the few non-Indo-European languages in Europe.
Hungarian stands out for three key reasons:

rows: Related to Finnish and Estonian, NOT to neighboring Slavic or Germanic languages
Words are built by stacking suffixes (one word can be a whole sentence!)
Suffixes change their vowels to match the root word

children.children: How did a Uralic language end up in Central Europe? The Magyar tribes migrated from the Ural Mountains region around 895 AD, settling in the Carpathian Basin. Despite 1000+ years surrounded by Indo-European languages, Hungarian kept its unique structure.
Hungarian has 44 letters! This includes 14 vowels and unique digraphs (two-letter combinations that represent single sounds).
Key pronunciation rules:
The accent mark (´) indicates a LONG vowel, not stress. This changes meaning! Compare: kor (age) vs kór (disease), hal (fish) vs hál (sleeps over). Always pronounce accented vowels longer.
Hungarian vowels are divided into two groups that don't like to mix:
Rule: Suffixes must match the vowel type of the root word!
Examples with the suffix 'in' (-ban/-ben):
Notice 'angolul' ends in -ul? That's the 'in [language]' suffix, following back vowel harmony with angol (English).
Watch how a single Hungarian word can express what English needs multiple words for:

Megszentségteleníthetetlenségeskedéseitekért means 'for your (plural) repeated pretending to be unable to be desecrated.' It's 44 letters—one for each letter in the alphabet! While artificial, it shows the language's agglutinative power.
Fun fact: Hungarian uses 'kettő' for standalone '2' but 'két' before nouns (két alma = two apples).

In English, word order tells you who does what: 'The dog sees the cat' means something very different from 'The cat sees the dog.' Hungarian works differently — it attaches suffixes (called case endings) to nouns to mark their grammatical role. This means word order becomes flexible and is used for emphasis rather than meaning.
Hungarian has approximately 18 cases, but don't panic! We'll start with the two most essential ones: the accusative and the dative.

terms.definition: A suffix added to a noun to show its role in a sentence (subject, object, recipient, location, etc.)
The case marking the direct object — the thing being acted upon. In Hungarian, the suffix is -t.
The case marking the indirect object — the person or thing something is given to or done for. In Hungarian, the suffix is -nak or -nek.
A word-ending added to change meaning or grammatical function. Hungarian stacks multiple suffixes on a single word (agglutination).

children.children: The Accusative (-t): Marking the Direct Object
The accusative case answers the question 'What?' or 'Whom?' — it marks the thing being seen, eaten, heard, loved, etc. The suffix is -t, but how you attach it depends on the word's ending.

rows: Noun ends in a consonant (back vowel word)
Noun ends in a consonant (front vowel word, non-rounded)
Noun ends in a consonant (front vowel word, rounded ö/ü)

children.children: Notice how the linking vowel (-ot, -et, -öt) follows vowel harmony? The same principle from Section 3 applies here! Back vowel words get -ot, front vowel words get -et or -öt.
The Dative (-nak/-nek): Marking the Indirect Object
The dative case answers the question 'To whom?' or 'For whom?' — it marks the recipient of an action. The suffix is -nak for back vowel words and -nek for front vowel words.

Full sentence example: 'Adok egy könyvet Marinak' (pronounced: AH-dok edj KӨNYH-vet MO-ree-nok) — I give a book to Mari. Here, 'könyvet' is accusative (the thing given) and 'Marinak' is dative (the person receiving).

props.title: Word Order Freedom — Cases in Action

children.children:
Because cases mark grammatical roles, Hungarian word order is flexible. These sentences all mean 'I see the dog,' but with different emphasis:

• Látom a kutyát. (LAH-tom ah KOO-tyaht) — neutral statement
• A kutyát látom. (ah KOO-tyaht LAH-tom) — emphasis on THE DOG (not the cat!)
• A kutyát én látom. (ah KOO-tyaht ayn LAH-tom) — emphasis on I see the dog (not you!)

The -t suffix on 'kutyát' always marks it as the object, no matter where it appears in the sentence. Without the case ending, Hungarian speakers wouldn't know who's seeing whom!

rows: I see the house. (emphasis on house)
I give a book to Mari.
I give a book to Mari. (emphasis on Mari)

children.children: Cases are like color-coded name tags at a party — no matter where someone stands in the room, you know their role by their tag. The subject wears a blue tag (no suffix needed — it's the default). The direct object wears a red tag (-t). The indirect object wears a green tag (-nak/-nek). You can rearrange people in the room however you like, and everyone still knows who's who.

English uses prepositions — small words like 'in,' 'on,' 'to,' 'for,' 'from,' 'with.' Hungarian fuses these meanings directly onto the noun as suffixes. Compare:

• English: in the house → Hungarian: házban (ház + -ban)
• English: to the house → Hungarian: házhoz (ház + -hoz)
• English: from the house → Hungarian: házból (ház + -ból)
• English: on the house → Hungarian: házon (ház + -on)
• English: for the friend → Hungarian: barátnak (barát + -nak)

So Hungarian's 18 cases aren't really scarier than English's many prepositions — they're just packaged differently. Instead of separate words floating before the noun, the meaning is welded onto the end. Once you see cases as 'attached prepositions,' they feel much less alien!

questions.question: What is the accusative (direct object) form of 'ház' (house)?
questions.explanation: 'Ház' ends in a consonant and is a back vowel word (á), so we add -at to form 'házat.' The -t suffix marks it as the direct object.

questions.question: Which suffix marks the dative case for a front vowel word like 'gyerek' (child)?
questions.explanation: 'Gyerek' contains front vowels (e, e), so it takes the front vowel variant of the dative suffix: -nek → gyereknek (to/for a child).

questions.question: Why can 'A kutyát látom' and 'Látom a kutyát' both mean 'I see the dog'?
options: Because Hungarian has no grammar rules
Because the accusative -t on 'kutyát' marks it as the object regardless of position
Because 'látom' changes meaning based on position
Because 'a' is a special word that controls meaning
questions.explanation: The -t suffix on 'kutyát' marks it as the direct object no matter where it appears in the sentence. Word order in Hungarian signals emphasis, not grammatical role.

questions.question: What is the accusative form of 'alma' (apple)?
questions.explanation: 'Alma' already ends in a vowel (-a), so we simply add -t. The final -a lengthens to -á, giving us 'almát' (pronounced AHL-maht).

children.children: Hungarian has TWO complete conjugation systems: indefinite (alanyi ragozás) and definite (tárgyas ragozás). The one you use depends on whether the object of the verb is specific or not. This is one of Hungarian's most distinctive features!

In English, 'I read' stays the same no matter what you're reading. In Hungarian, the verb itself changes form depending on whether you're talking about a specific object or not. It's as if English had two completely different sets of verb endings — one for 'I read (something)' and another for 'I read IT / I read THE book.'
Indefinite Conjugation (alanyi ragozás)
Use the indefinite conjugation when there is no object, or the object is unspecified/indefinite:
Definite Conjugation (tárgyas ragozás)
Use the definite conjugation when the object is specific or definite:
Present Tense Conjugation of 'olvas' (to read) — Both Systems

It's like English 'I read' vs 'I read IT' — but Hungarian marks this distinction right on the verb itself. Imagine every verb has two wardrobes of outfits: one for when you're talking vaguely ('I'm reading...'), and another for when you know exactly what ('I'm reading THE book'). English doesn't change the verb at all, but Hungarian swaps the entire ending.
When to Use Which? — Quick Reference

rows: Látok egy kutyát. (I see a dog.)
Látom a kutyát. (I see the dog.)
Olvasom a könyvedet. (I read your book.)

props.title: The Same Verb, Two Conjugations — Side by Side

children.children:
Watch how 'lát' (to see) changes depending on the object:

• Látok. (LAH-tok) — I see. (no object → indefinite)
• Látok egy madarat. (LAH-tok edj MAH-dah-rot) — I see a bird. ('egy' → indefinite)
• Látom a madarat. (LAH-tom ah MAH-dah-rot) — I see THE bird. ('a' → definite)
• Látom Pétert. (LAH-tom PAY-tert) — I see Péter. (proper name → definite)
• Látom azt. (LAH-tom ozt) — I see that. ('azt' → definite)
• Mit látsz? (meet lahts) — What do you see? ('mit' → indefinite)

Notice how the ending flips between -ok/-om, telling the listener whether you mean 'a bird' or 'THE bird' — information carried entirely by the verb!

props.title: Does Any Other Language Do This?

children.children: Yes, but it's rare! This feature is called 'objective conjugation' or 'definite conjugation' by linguists. Basque (spoken in Spain/France) has a similar system where verbs agree with their objects. Mordvin languages (Erzya and Moksha, Uralic relatives of Hungarian spoken in Russia) also distinguish definite and indefinite conjugations. A few other Uralic languages show traces of it too. But Hungarian's system is among the most fully developed and consistently used in everyday speech — every single transitive verb in the language has both complete conjugation sets.

questions.question: Which conjugation would you use for: 'Olvas___ egy újságot' (I read a newspaper)?
questions.explanation: 'Egy újságot' uses 'egy' (a/an), making the object indefinite. So you use the indefinite conjugation: 'Olvasok egy újságot.'

questions.question: You want to say 'I see Péter.' Which form of 'lát' (to see) do you use for 'I'?
questions.explanation: Péter is a proper name, which always triggers the definite conjugation. So it's 'Látom Pétert' — with the definite first-person ending -om.

questions.question: Why does 'Látom' (with no other words) already imply a specific object?
options: It doesn't — it means 'I see' with no object
The definite ending -om signals that there's a specific 'it' being seen
'-om' is just the default first-person ending
It's a fixed expression with no grammatical meaning
questions.explanation: The definite conjugation ending -om already encodes 'I [verb] IT/THAT.' By saying 'Látom' you're saying 'I see it' — the specific object is implied by the verb form itself.

questions.question: 'Azt olvas___.' (I read that.) — Which ending fits?
questions.explanation: 'Azt' (that) is a definite pronoun — it refers to something specific. So you must use the definite conjugation: 'Azt olvasom.'

props.title: 9. Possession Suffixes — "My, Your, His" Built Into the Word

children.children: Hungarian doesn't use separate possessive pronouns like English 'my book.' Instead, it adds suffixes directly to the noun: 'könyvem' (KӨNYH-vem) = 'my book' (könyv + -em). One word does the job of two!

In English, you say 'my house,' 'your house,' 'his house' — two words every time. Hungarian builds the possessor right into the noun with a suffix. 'Ház' (house) becomes 'házam' (my house), 'házad' (your house), 'háza' (his/her house). The personal pronoun (én, te, ő...) is usually dropped because the suffix already tells you who the owner is!
Here are all the possessive suffixes using the back vowel word 'ház' (house, pronounced HAHZ):
Vowel Harmony in Possessive Suffixes

Just like every other suffix in Hungarian, possessive endings obey vowel harmony. Back vowel words (like 'ház') get back vowel suffixes, and front vowel words get front vowel suffixes. Compare 'ház' (house — back vowel) with 'kert' (garden — front vowel, pronounced KEHRT):

Notice the -j- in 'kertje' (his/her garden) and 'kertjük' (their garden)? Many nouns insert a -j- in the third person forms. Whether the -j- appears depends on the specific noun — it's one of the trickier parts of possession to memorize!

props.title: Building Possessed Forms Step by Step

children.children:
Let's see how this works with 'könyv' (book, pronounced KӨNYV — a front vowel word with rounded ö):

• könyv + -em → könyvem (KӨNYH-vem) — my book
• könyv + -ed → könyved (KӨNYH-ved) — your book
• könyv + -e → könyve (KӨNYH-veh) — his/her book
• könyv + -ünk → könyvünk (KӨNYH-vünk) — our book
• könyv + -etek → könyvetek (KӨNYH-veh-tek) — your (pl.) book
• könyv + -ük → könyvük (KӨNYH-vük) — their book

And you can keep stacking! Add the accusative -et and you get:
• könyvemet (KӨNYH-veh-met) — my book (as object): 'Olvasom a könyvemet.' = 'I'm reading my book.'

Imagine if English contracted 'my book' into a single word — 'bookmy' or 'mybook.' That's essentially what Hungarian does naturally! 'Könyvem' is literally 'book-my' fused into one word. And because Hungarian stacks suffixes, you can keep going: 'könyveimben' = 'in my books' — that's book + my + plural + in, all in one word. It's like saying 'booksmyin' as a single unit!

props.title: What About 'the X of Y'? — Péter háza

children.children:
To express 'Peter's house' in Hungarian, you say 'Péter háza' (PAY-ter HAH-zah). Notice something surprising: the possessive suffix -a goes on 'ház' (the possessed thing), NOT on 'Péter' (the possessor)! The possessed noun always gets the suffix.

More examples:
• a tanár könyve (ah TAH-nahr KӨNYH-veh) — the teacher's book (literally: 'the teacher book-his/her')
• az anya háza (ahz AH-nyah HAH-zah) — the mother's house
• Mari kutyája (MO-ree KOO-tyah-yah) — Mari's dog ('kutya' + -ja)

Optionally, the possessor can take a -nak/-nek suffix too: 'Péternek a háza' — this emphasizes the possessor and is common in longer sentences. But the possessed noun ALWAYS gets its possessive suffix regardless.

This is the opposite of English's apostrophe-s, which goes on the owner ('Peter's'). In Hungarian, the owned thing is marked instead!

questions.question: How do you say 'my house' in Hungarian?
questions.explanation: Hungarian builds possession into the noun with a suffix. 'Ház' + '-am' (my, for back vowel words) = 'házam.' No separate pronoun needed!

questions.question: What is the correct possessive form for 'our garden' using 'kert' (garden)?
questions.explanation: 'Kert' is a front vowel word, so the 'we' suffix uses the front vowel variant: -ünk → 'kertünk' (our garden).

questions.question: In 'Péter háza' (Peter's house), which word carries the possessive suffix?
options: Péter — the possessor gets the suffix
háza — the possessed noun gets the suffix
questions.explanation: In Hungarian, the POSSESSED noun (the thing being owned) gets the possessive suffix. 'Háza' = 'ház' + '-a' (his/her). The possessor 'Péter' stays unmarked (or optionally takes -nek).

questions.question: Why does 'kertje' (his/her garden) have a -j- but 'háza' (his/her house) does not?
options: Because 'kert' is a front vowel word
Whether -j- appears in 3rd person depends on the specific noun — it must be memorized
Because 'kert' ends in a consonant cluster
questions.explanation: The insertion of -j- in third person possessive forms is noun-specific and must be learned for each word. Some nouns take it (kertje, kutyája), others don't (háza, ablaka). This is one of the trickier aspects of Hungarian possession.

props.title: 10. Word Order — Topic, Focus, and the Verb

children.children: Hungarian is often called a 'free word order' language. This is misleading! Every word order is grammatically valid, but each arrangement means something DIFFERENT. Word order is how Hungarian expresses emphasis, contrast, and new information.

In English, you change emphasis with your voice: 'PETER saw the cat' vs 'Peter saw THE CAT.' In Hungarian, you change emphasis by MOVING WORDS. The position of each word relative to the verb tells the listener exactly what's important.

items.definition: What the sentence is ABOUT — old, known information. Usually comes first.
The NEW or EMPHASIZED information — the answer to an unspoken question. Sits immediately BEFORE the verb.
The action — its position is anchored by what comes before it (the focus).

children.children: The basic structure is: TOPIC → FOCUS → VERB → the rest. Let's break each piece down.

props.title: The Topic — What We're Talking About

children.children: The topic is the 'old news' — what the sentence is about. It usually comes at the very beginning. Think of it as setting the scene.
A macska... (AH MOTCH-kah) — 'The cat...' This tells the listener: we're talking about the cat. Whatever comes next is the new information about it.
Péter... (PAY-ter) — 'Peter...' Now we know the sentence is about Peter. The important part is still coming.
Tegnap... (TEG-nop) — 'Yesterday...' Sets the time frame. The listener knows we're talking about yesterday; now tell me what happened!

This is the most important concept in Hungarian word order. The FOCUS is the word placed immediately before the verb. It receives the main stress of the sentence and carries the new, emphasized, or contrasted information.
Think of the spot right before the verb as a SPOTLIGHT. Whatever word you place there becomes the star of the sentence — the piece of information you're highlighting.
PÉTER látta a macskát. (PAY-ter LAAT-tah ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'It was PETER who saw the cat.' (Not someone else — Peter!)
A MACSKÁT látta Péter. (ah MOTCH-kaat LAAT-tah PAY-ter) — 'It was THE CAT that Peter saw.' (Not the dog — the cat!)
TEGNAP látta Péter a macskát. (TEG-nop LAAT-tah PAY-ter ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'It was YESTERDAY that Peter saw the cat.' (Not today — yesterday!)

rows: It was PETER who saw the cat.
It was THE CAT that Peter saw.
It was YESTERDAY that Peter saw the cat.
A KERTBEN látta Péter a macskát.
ah KERT-ben LAAT-tah PAY-ter ah MOTCH-kaat
It was IN THE GARDEN that Peter saw the cat.

children.children: Notice how the SAME words appear in every sentence above — only the ORDER changes! And each order creates a completely different emphasis. This is the magic of Hungarian word order.
A neutral sentence has no special emphasis — it's just a plain statement of fact. In neutral order, the verb often comes second, and no single element is stressed.
Neutral: Péter látta a macskát. (PAY-ter LAAT-tah ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'Peter saw the cat.' (Just reporting what happened.)
Focused: PÉTER látta a macskát. (PAY-ter LAAT-tah ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'It was PETER who saw the cat.' (Someone asks 'Who saw the cat?' — The answer is Peter, so Peter goes in the focus spot.)

In writing, neutral and focused look the same when the subject happens to already be before the verb. The difference is in SPOKEN stress. But when you focus a different element, the word order visibly changes!
When you negate a sentence, the word 'nem' (NEM — not) MUST go immediately before the verb. Negation is always focused — it's always the most important information.
Péter NEM látta a macskát. (PAY-ter NEM LAAT-tah ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'Peter did NOT see the cat.'
A macskát NEM látta Péter. (ah MOTCH-kaat NEM LAAT-tah PAY-ter) — 'The cat, Peter did NOT see.' (The cat is the topic, the negation is the focus.)
NEM Péter látta a macskát. (NEM PAY-ter LAAT-tah ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'It was NOT Peter who saw the cat.' (Here 'nem' negates the focus element 'Péter' — someone else did!)
Notice: 'nem' always clings to the verb or to the focused word it negates. You can NEVER separate 'nem' from what it negates.

props.title: Question Words Are Always in Focus

children.children: Question words (who, what, when, where, why, how) are inherently focused — they ask for NEW information. So they must go in the focus position, immediately before the verb.
KI látta a macskát? (KEE LAAT-tah ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'WHO saw the cat?'
MIT látott Péter? (MEET LAA-tott PAY-ter) — 'WHAT did Peter see?'
MIKOR látta Péter a macskát? (MEE-kor LAAT-tah PAY-ter ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'WHEN did Peter see the cat?'
HOL látta Péter a macskát? (HOLE LAAT-tah PAY-ter ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'WHERE did Peter see the cat?'
MIÉRT nézte Péter a macskát? (MEE-ayrt NAYZ-teh PAY-ter ah MOTCH-kaat) — 'WHY was Peter looking at the cat?'
Pattern: question word + verb + everything else. This is ALWAYS the rule. You cannot put anything between a question word and its verb in Hungarian!

props.explanation: Imagine a stage where the verb is the director. Right in front of the director is a single spotlight. In English, the spotlight is invisible — you shine it with your voice (stress and intonation). In Hungarian, the spotlight is a PHYSICAL POSITION. To emphasize a word, you literally MOVE it into the spotlight (the slot right before the verb). The audience (listener) always knows what's important because they can see what's standing in the light.

items.title: Step 1: Identify what the sentence is ABOUT
items.description: This is your topic. Put it at the beginning. Example: 'A macskát...' (AH MOTCH-kaat) — we're talking about the cat.

items.title: Step 2: Decide what's NEW or EMPHASIZED
items.description: This is your focus. It goes immediately before the verb. If the answer to 'who?' is Péter, then Péter is your focus.

items.title: Step 3: Place the verb right after the focus
items.description: The verb follows the focused element with no gap. 'PÉTER látta' (PAY-ter LAAT-tah) — focus + verb, glued together.

items.title: Step 4: Everything else goes after the verb
items.description: The remaining elements follow in relatively free order. 'PÉTER látta a macskát tegnap a kertben.' (PAY-ter LAAT-tah ah MOTCH-kaat TEG-nop ah KERT-ben)

props.title: Spot the Focus!
props.description: In each Hungarian sentence below, identify which word or phrase is in the FOCUS position (immediately before the verb). Remember: the focus carries the main emphasis!

children.children: 1. ANNA vette meg a könyvet. (ON-nah VET-teh meg ah KUNYV-et) → Focus: ANNA — 'It was ANNA who bought the book.'
2. A KÖNYVET vette meg Anna. (ah KUNYV-et VET-teh meg ON-nah) → Focus: A KÖNYVET — 'It was THE BOOK that Anna bought.'
3. TEGNAP vette meg Anna a könyvet. (TEG-nop VET-teh meg ON-nah ah KUNYV-et) → Focus: TEGNAP — 'It was YESTERDAY that Anna bought the book.'
4. Anna NEM vette meg a könyvet. (ON-nah NEM VET-teh meg ah KUNYV-et) → Focus: NEM (negation) — 'Anna did NOT buy the book.'
5. KI vette meg a könyvet? (KEE VET-teh meg ah KUNYV-et) → Focus: KI (question word) — 'WHO bought the book?'

props.title: Rearrange for Different Meanings
props.description: Given the words: 'Mari', 'szereti' (loves), 'a csokoládét' (the chocolate) — how would you arrange them to create these meanings?

children.children: Words: Mari (MAH-ree) | szereti (SEH-reh-tee, 'loves') | a csokoládét (ah CHO-ko-laa-dayt, 'the chocolate')
Q: 'Mari loves chocolate.' (neutral statement) → A: Mari szereti a csokoládét. (MAH-ree SEH-reh-tee ah CHO-ko-laa-dayt)
Q: 'It is MARI who loves chocolate.' (emphasize Mari) → A: MARI szereti a csokoládét. (MAH-ree SEH-reh-tee ah CHO-ko-laa-dayt) — Mari is in focus before the verb.
Q: 'It is CHOCOLATE that Mari loves.' (emphasize chocolate) → A: A CSOKOLÁDÉT szereti Mari. (ah CHO-ko-laa-dayt SEH-reh-tee MAH-ree) — Chocolate moves to focus position.
Q: 'Mari does NOT love chocolate.' (negate) → A: Mari NEM szereti a csokoládét. (MAH-ree NEM SEH-reh-tee ah CHO-ko-laa-dayt) — Negation takes focus.
Q: 'WHO loves chocolate?' (question) → A: KI szereti a csokoládét? (KEE SEH-reh-tee ah CHO-ko-laa-dayt) — Question word in focus.

props.question: In the sentence 'A KUTYÁT kergette a macska' (AH KOO-tyaat KER-get-teh ah MOTCH-kah), what is being emphasized?
options: The cat (a macska) — it's at the end so it must be important
The dog (a kutyát) — it's in focus position, right before the verb
The chasing (kergette) — verbs are always the focus
Nothing — this is a neutral sentence
props.explanation: 'A kutyát' (the dog) sits immediately before the verb 'kergette' (chased), putting it in the FOCUS position. The sentence means 'It was THE DOG that the cat chased' — not the bird, not the mouse, but the DOG. The cat (a macska) is just the topic.

props.question: How would you say 'WHO ate the cake?' in Hungarian, given: ki (who), ette (ate), meg (verbal prefix), a tortát (the cake)?
props.explanation: Question words MUST be in focus position — immediately before the verb. 'KI ette meg a tortát?' (KEE ET-teh meg ah TOR-taat) — 'WHO ate the cake?' The question word 'ki' sits right before the verb 'ette', exactly where it belongs.

props.question: What does moving 'nem' (not) in front of a specific word do? Compare: 'Péter NEM látta a macskát' vs 'NEM Péter látta a macskát'.
options: They mean the same thing — word order doesn't matter for negation
The first negates the verb (Peter didn't see), the second negates Peter (it wasn't Peter who saw)
props.explanation: 'Péter NEM látta a macskát' (PAY-ter NEM LAAT-tah ah MOTCH-kaat) = 'Peter did NOT see the cat' — 'nem' negates the action. 'NEM Péter látta a macskát' (NEM PAY-ter LAAT-tah ah MOTCH-kaat) = 'It was NOT Peter who saw the cat' — 'nem' negates who did it. The position of 'nem' determines WHAT is being negated!

children.children: In English, we put little direction words BEFORE the noun: 'in the house', 'under the table', 'behind the door'. These are called prepositions (pre = before). Hungarian flips this completely — the direction word comes AFTER the noun. These are called postpositions (post = after). It's one of the most distinctive features of Hungarian, and once you get used to it, it's surprisingly logical!

🔄 THE BIG FLIP: English says 'under the table' (direction + noun). Hungarian says 'az asztal alatt' (oz OS-tol AH-lot) — literally 'the table under'. The noun always comes first, and the postposition follows as a SEPARATE word. Think of it as: tell me WHAT first, then tell me WHERE!

This isn't just a quirky grammar rule — it reflects how Hungarian organizes information. You name the thing first, then specify its spatial relationship. Compare: English 'under the big wooden table' vs. Hungarian 'a nagy fa asztal alatt' (AH NOJ FAH OS-tol AH-lot) — literally 'the big wooden table under'. The postposition 'alatt' sits calmly at the end, never attached to the noun.

rows: az asztal alatt (oz OS-tol AH-lot) — under the table
a fej fölött (AH FEY FŌ-lōtt) — above the head
a ház mellett (AH HAAZ MEL-lett) — beside the house
az ajtó mögött (oz OY-tō MŌ-gōtt) — behind the door
az iskola előtt (oz ISH-ko-lah EH-lōtt) — in front of the school
a fák között (AH FAAK KŌ-zōtt) — between the trees
tea helyett (TEH-ah HEH-yett) — instead of tea
az eső miatt (oz EH-shō MEE-ott) — because of the rain
a térkép szerint (AH TAYR-kayp SEH-rint) — according to the map

props.title: Postpositions vs. Case Suffixes — What's the Difference?

children.children: You might be wondering: didn't we already learn about spatial markers like -ban/-ben (in), -ra/-re (onto), and -ból/-ből (out of)? Those are case suffixes — they ATTACH directly to the noun as endings. Postpositions are SEPARATE words that follow the noun. Both express spatial relationships, but they work differently.

Case suffixes glue onto the noun: 'házban' (HAAZ-bon) = 'in the house' — one word. Postpositions stand alone after the noun: 'a ház mellett' (AH HAAZ MEL-lett) = 'beside the house' — two separate words. You can't say *házmellettben — that's mixing two systems!

Here's the pattern: the most common spatial concepts (in, on, to, from, into, onto) use case suffixes. The more specific or complex ones (under, beside, behind, between, instead of, because of) use postpositions. Think of suffixes as the 'inner circle' and postpositions as the 'outer circle' of spatial expression.

Hungarian spatial thinking revolves around THREE DIRECTIONS: 1) Where at? (static location), 2) Where to? (movement toward), 3) Where from? (movement away). Both suffixes and postpositions follow this three-way system. For suffixes: -ban (in) / -ba (into) / -ból (out of). For postpositions: alatt (under) / alá (to under) / alól (from under). This trio pattern is everywhere!

Now here's where it gets really elegant. Just as case suffixes come in triples (like -ban/-ba/-ból for 'in/into/out of'), many postpositions also have THREE forms: one for static location ('where at?'), one for direction toward ('where to?'), and one for direction away ('where from?'). This three-direction system is one of the most beautiful and logical features of Hungarian.

🔺 PATTERN ALERT: Notice the endings! Static forms often end in -tt (alatt, mögött, fölött). 'Toward' forms often end in a long vowel -á/-é (alá, mögé, fölé). 'Away from' forms often end in -l (alól, mögül, fölül). Once you spot this pattern, you can often guess the form you need!

props.analogy: Postpositions are like putting the address label AFTER the package contents. In English, you read the GPS instruction first, then the destination: 'Turn toward the store.' In Hungarian, you name the destination first, then the instruction: 'A bolt felé fordulj' (AH BOLT FEH-lay FOR-dooly) — literally 'the store toward turn.' You always know WHAT you're talking about before you learn WHERE it is relative to you.
props.explanation: English front-loads the spatial relationship (preposition first, then noun). Hungarian front-loads the noun (noun first, then postposition). It's like reading a shipping label: English puts 'TO:' before the address, Hungarian writes the address first and stamps 'TO' at the end. Both systems work — they just prioritize differently!

children.children: 🐱 'A macska az asztal alatt van.' (AH MOTCH-kah oz OS-tol AH-lot VON) — 'The cat is under the table.' The postposition 'alatt' follows 'asztal' (table) as a separate word.
📖 'A könyv a szék mögött van.' (AH KŌNYV AH SAYK MŌ-gōtt VON) — 'The book is behind the chair.' Notice 'mögött' comes right after 'szék' (chair).
👧👦 'A gyerekek a ház előtt játszanak.' (AH DYEH-reh-kek AH HAAZ EH-lōtt YAAT-sah-nok) — 'The children are playing in front of the house.' Here 'előtt' (in front of) follows 'ház' (house).

🐕 'A kutya a fa alá fut.' (AH KOO-tyah AH FAH AH-laa FOOT) — 'The dog runs to under the tree.' This uses the DIRECTIONAL form 'alá' (toward under) instead of the static 'alatt' (at under), because the dog is moving TO that position!
🐦 'A madár a fészek fölé repül.' (AH MOD-aar AH FAY-sek FŌ-lay REH-pūl) — 'The bird flies to above the nest.' Again, the directional 'fölé' (toward above) shows movement, not a static position.

🚗 'Az autó a garázsból a ház mögé állt.' (OZ OW-tō AH GOH-raazh-bōl AH HAAZ MŌ-gay AALLT) — 'The car moved from the garage to behind the house.' Here you see a case suffix (-ból = from) AND a postposition (mögé = to behind) working together in the same sentence!

props.title: Place the Postposition!
props.description: Hungarian postpositions come AFTER the noun. Given the English sentence, think about which Hungarian postposition you'd use and where it goes. Remember: noun first, postposition second — always as a separate word!

children.children: 🧩 Challenge 1: 'The cat is BEHIND the sofa.' → Think: What's the noun? (sofa = kanapé, KOH-noh-pay). What's the postposition? (behind = mögött, MŌ-gōtt). Answer: 'A macska a kanapé mögött van.' (AH MOTCH-kah AH KOH-noh-pay MŌ-gōtt VON)

🧩 Challenge 2: 'The dog ran TO BEHIND the tree.' → This needs the directional form! (tree = fa, FAH). Static 'behind' = mögött, but 'to behind' = mögé (MŌ-gay). Answer: 'A kutya a fa mögé futott.' (AH KOO-tyah AH FAH MŌ-gay FOO-tott)
🧩 Challenge 3: 'I drink coffee WITHOUT sugar.' → (coffee = kávé, KAA-vay; sugar = cukor, TSOO-kor; without = nélkül, NAYL-kūl). Answer: 'Kávét iszom cukor nélkül.' (KAA-vayt EE-som TSOO-kor NAYL-kūl)

🧩 Challenge 4: 'The ball rolled FROM UNDER the bed.' → This needs the 'away from' form! (bed = ágy, AADJ). Static 'under' = alatt, but 'from under' = alól (AH-lōl). Answer: 'A labda az ágy alól gurult ki.' (AH LOB-dah oz AADJ AH-lōl GOO-roolt KEE)

props.question: How would you say 'beside the river' in Hungarian? (river = folyó, FOY-ō)
props.explanation: 'A folyó mellett' (AH FOY-ō MEL-lett) is correct! 'Mellett' means 'beside' (static location). Option A uses 'mellé' which means 'to beside' (movement toward). Option C puts the postposition before the noun — that's English word order, not Hungarian! Option D glues the postposition onto the noun — but postpositions are always separate words (unlike case suffixes).

props.question: The cat crawled FROM UNDER the bed. Which postposition form do you need? (bed = ágy, AADJ)
props.explanation: You need 'alól' (AH-lōl) — the 'from under' form! The cat is moving AWAY FROM under the bed. 'Alatt' would mean the cat is just sitting under it (static). 'Alá' would mean the cat is going TO under it (toward). The three-direction system: alatt (where at?) / alá (where to?) / alól (where from?). Full sentence: 'A macska az ágy alól mászott ki.' (AH MOTCH-kah oz AADJ AH-lōl MAA-shott KEE)

props.question: Which of these is NOT a postposition but a case suffix?
props.explanation: '-ban/-ben' is a case suffix, not a postposition! It attaches directly to the noun: 'házban' (HAAZ-bon) = 'in the house' — one word. The others (mellett, között, miatt) are all postpositions — separate words that follow the noun. Remember: suffixes attach, postpositions stand alone!

children.children: You can describe things, attach suffixes, and even order your words for emphasis. But what about asking questions? Hungarian has a beautifully systematic approach to question formation — from simple intonation changes to an elegant question particle that removes all ambiguity.

Hungarian has two main types of questions: yes/no questions (eldöntendő kérdés, EL-dōn-ten-dō KAYR-daysh) and information questions using question words (kiegészítendő kérdés, KEE-eh-gay-SEE-ten-dō KAYR-daysh). Each type has its own elegant tools!

items.title: Yes/No Questions (Eldöntendő kérdés)
items.description: Questions expecting 'igen' (yes) or 'nem' (no). Formed by intonation, the -e particle, or the tag word 'ugye'. Example: 'Szereted a csokoládét?' (SEH-reh-ted oh CHO-ko-laa-dayt?) = 'Do you like chocolate?'

items.title: Information Questions (Kiegészítendő kérdés)
items.description: Questions using question words like ki? (who?), mi? (what?), hol? (where?). These demand a specific answer, not just yes or no. Example: 'Ki szereti a csokoládét?' (KEE SEH-reh-tee oh CHO-ko-laa-dayt?) = 'Who likes chocolate?'

children.children: Let's start with the simplest way to ask a question in Hungarian — just change your voice!
The easiest way to form a yes/no question? Just raise your intonation at the end of the sentence. The words stay exactly the same!
Statement: 'Péter itt van.' (PAY-ter ITT VON.) = 'Peter is here.' ➜ Question: 'Péter itt van?' (PAY-ter ITT VON?) = 'Is Peter here?' — Same words, rising pitch at the end!
Statement: 'Szereted a kávét.' (SEH-reh-ted oh KAA-vayt.) = 'You like coffee.' ➜ Question: 'Szereted a kávét?' (SEH-reh-ted oh KAA-vayt?) = 'Do you like coffee?'
Statement: 'Mari otthon van.' (MO-ree OTT-hon VON.) = 'Mari is at home.' ➜ Question: 'Mari otthon van?' (MO-ree OTT-hon VON?) = 'Is Mari at home?'
⚠️ In speech, this works perfectly. But in writing, only the question mark tells you it's a question — which is why Hungarian developed the -e particle!
Intonation-only questions are casual and conversational. But what if you're writing, or want to be crystal clear? Enter the -e particle.

items.definition: A short particle attached to the verb (or focused word) with a hyphen. It transforms any statement into an unambiguous yes/no question, even in writing. Always pronounced as a short, unstressed 'eh' after the word it attaches to.
The -e particle attaches to the VERB in neutral sentences, or to the FOCUSED word if there is one. It never floats freely — it must be hyphenated to the word it modifies.

children.children: 'Péter itt van-e?' (PAY-ter ITT VON-eh?) = 'Is Peter here?' — The -e attaches to 'van' (the verb), making the question explicit.
'Tudod-e a választ?' (TOO-dod-eh oh VAA-lost?) = 'Do you know the answer?' — The -e attaches to 'tudod' (the verb).
'Szép-e a ház?' (SAYP-eh oh HAAZ?) = 'Is the house beautiful?' — Here -e attaches to the focused adjective 'szép', not the verb!
'El tudod-e olvasni?' (EL TOO-dod-eh OL-vosh-nee?) = 'Can you read it?' — With verb prefixes, -e goes after the conjugated verb part.

The -e particle is especially useful in formal writing, reported speech ('Megkérdezte, szép-e a ház' = 'He asked whether the house was beautiful'), and embedded questions. In casual speech, intonation is usually enough!
'Ugye' (OO-dyeh) works like English 'right?', 'isn't it?', or 'don't you think?'. It's added to statements when you expect the listener to agree.
'Péter itt van, ugye?' (PAY-ter ITT VON, OO-dyeh?) = 'Peter is here, right?'
'Szép az idő, ugye?' (SAYP oz EE-dō, OO-dyeh?) = 'The weather is nice, isn't it?'
'Ugye eljössz?' (OO-dyeh EL-yōss?) = 'You'll come, won't you?' — 'Ugye' can also go at the BEGINNING for gentle emphasis or mild pressure!
'Ugye' is extremely common in everyday Hungarian conversation. It softens questions and invites agreement — think of it as the Swiss Army knife of tag questions.
Now let's move to the other major type: information questions. These use specific question words — and Hungarian has a wonderfully complete set of them.

rows: Ki ez? (KEE EZ?) = Who is this?
Mi ez? (MEE EZ?) = What is this?
Hol van? (HOHL VON?) = Where is it?
Mikor jössz? (MEE-kor YŌSS?) = When are you coming?
Miért sírsz? (MEE-ayrt SHEERSS?) = Why are you crying?
Hogy vagy? (HODJ VODJ?) = How are you?
Melyik a tied? (MEH-yik oh TEE-ed?) = Which one is yours?
Mennyi az idő? (MEN-nyee oz EE-dō?) = What time is it?
Hány éves vagy? (HAHNY AY-vesh VODJ?) = How old are you?
Kié ez a könyv? (KEE-ay EZ oh KŌNYV?) = Whose book is this?

children.children: Notice anything about placement? In every single example above, the question word comes IMMEDIATELY before the verb. This is not optional — it's mandatory!

props.title: Question Words in Focus Position — The Iron Rule

children.children: Remember Section 10 on word order? Question words are ALWAYS in focus position — directly before the verb. No exceptions!
✅ 'Ki látta a macskát?' (KEE LAAT-tah oh MOTCH-kaat?) = 'WHO saw the cat?' — 'Ki' is right before the verb 'látta'.
✅ 'Hol van Péter?' (HOHL VON PAY-ter?) = 'WHERE is Peter?' — 'Hol' is right before the verb 'van'.
✅ 'Mit csinálsz?' (MEET CHEE-naals?) = 'WHAT are you doing?' — 'Mit' (accusative of 'mi') is right before the verb.
❌ NEVER: *'Péter hol van?' — The question word MUST precede the verb directly. Péter gets pushed elsewhere.

❌ NEVER: *'A macskát ki látta?' — While the object can be topicalized, the question word still must be immediately before the verb, so the correct topicalized form would be: 'A macskát ki látta?' — actually, this IS correct because 'ki' is still right before 'látta'! The rule is about the question word + verb bond, not absolute sentence position.
In Section 10, we learned that Hungarian word order follows a Topic–Focus–Verb pattern. Question words are the ultimate focus elements — they MUST occupy the focus position directly before the verb.

This is why question words 'steal' the focus slot: 'Péter megevett három almát.' (PAY-ter MEG-eh-vett HAA-rom OL-maat) = 'Peter ate three apples.' ➜ 'Ki evett meg három almát?' (KEE EH-vett MEG HAA-rom OL-maat?) = 'Who ate three apples?' Notice the verb prefix 'meg-' separates and moves AFTER the verb when a question word takes focus — just like with other focused elements!
This prefix-splitting behavior is your reliable signal that focus is at work. If you see a separated verb prefix, something important is in the focus slot — often a question word!
One of Hungarian's cleverest features is how it handles 'where' — not as one word, but THREE, each encoding a different relationship with location.

rows: Hol van a kutya? (HOHL VON oh KOO-tyah?) = Where is the dog?
Hová mész? (HOH-vaa MAYS?) = Where are you going?
Honnan jössz? (HON-non YŌSS?) = Where are you coming from?

props.analogy: Think of a bus stop. 'Hol?' asks where you ARE (standing at the stop). 'Hová?' asks where you're GOING (getting on the bus). 'Honnan?' asks where you CAME FROM (where you boarded).
props.explanation: This three-way distinction mirrors the three-direction system in Hungarian postpositions (Section 11) and case suffixes. Hungarian always cares about whether something is static, moving toward, or moving away. It's not just a question word quirk — it's a fundamental feature of how Hungarian encodes space and direction across the entire language.

children.children: This three-way 'where' system is echoed throughout Hungarian: 'itt/ide/innen' (here/to here/from here), 'ott/oda/onnan' (there/to there/from there), and even in case suffixes like '-ban/-ba/-ból' (in/into/out of). Once you internalize this pattern, a huge chunk of Hungarian grammar clicks into place!

props.title: Answering Questions — Igen, Nem, and the Verb Echo

children.children: The basics: 'Igen' (EE-gen) = 'Yes'. 'Nem' (NEM) = 'No'. But Hungarian has a more natural way to answer — echo the verb!
'Beszélsz magyarul?' (BEH-sayls MOD-yah-rool?) = 'Do you speak Hungarian?' ➜ 'Beszélek.' (BEH-say-lek.) = 'I speak.' — The conjugated verb alone is a complete, natural answer!
'Szereted a pizzát?' (SEH-reh-ted oh PEETS-tsaat?) = 'Do you like pizza?' ➜ 'Szeretem.' (SEH-reh-tem.) = 'I like (it).' — Notice the definite conjugation, since 'pizza' is specific!
'Eljössz holnap?' (EL-yōss HOHL-nop?) = 'Will you come tomorrow?' ➜ 'Eljövök.' (EL-yō-vōk.) = 'I'll come.' — The verb prefix reattaches in the affirmative answer.
For negative answers, add 'nem' before the verb: 'Nem beszélek.' (NEM BEH-say-lek.) = 'I don't speak.' or just 'Nem.' for a blunt no.

props.title: Form the Question!
props.description: Practice turning statements into questions and choosing the right question words. Look at each statement or English prompt, then think about which Hungarian question formation strategy you'd use.

children.children: Challenge 1: Turn this statement into a yes/no question using the -e particle: 'Péter beszél angolul.' (PAY-ter BEH-sayl ON-go-lool.) = 'Peter speaks English.' ➜ Answer: 'Péter beszél-e angolul?' (PAY-ter BEH-sayl-eh ON-go-lool?) = 'Does Peter speak English?'
Challenge 2: Translate to Hungarian: 'Where are you going?' Think: movement TOWARD a place → use 'Hová?' ➜ Answer: 'Hová mész?' (HOH-vaa MAYS?)
Challenge 3: Translate to Hungarian: 'Who is cooking?' Think: question word 'ki' + verb in focus position ➜ Answer: 'Ki főz?' (KEE FŌZ?)
Challenge 4: Turn into a tag question: 'Jó a film.' (YOH oh FILM.) = 'The movie is good.' ➜ Answer: 'Jó a film, ugye?' (YOH oh FILM, OO-dyeh?) = 'The movie is good, right?'
Challenge 5: Translate to Hungarian: 'Where did you come from?' Think: movement FROM a place → use 'Honnan?' ➜ Answer: 'Honnan jöttél?' (HON-non YŌT-tayl?)

props.question: Which question word would you use to ask 'Where are you going?' in Hungarian?
props.explanation: 'Hová?' (HOH-vaa?) is the correct choice because you're asking about movement TOWARD a destination. 'Hol?' would be for a static location ('Where ARE you?'), and 'Honnan?' would be for origin ('Where are you coming FROM?'). Hungarian's three-way 'where' system always distinguishes static position, direction toward, and direction from!

props.question: What is wrong with the sentence *'Péter hol van?' (intended: 'Where is Peter?')?
options: The question word 'hol' must be immediately before the verb 'van' (focus position)
It should use the -e particle instead
props.explanation: Question words MUST occupy the focus position — directly before the verb. The correct form is 'Hol van Péter?' (HOHL VON PAY-ter?), where 'hol' sits right before 'van'. This is the same focus-position rule from Section 10: the questioned element always gets the spotlight slot before the verb!

props.question: How would a native Hungarian speaker most naturally answer: 'Szereted a magyar konyhát?' (SEH-reh-ted oh MOD-yar KONY-haat?) = 'Do you like Hungarian cuisine?'
options: 'Igen szeretem.' (EE-gen SEH-reh-tem.) — Yes I like it.
'Szeretem.' (SEH-reh-tem.) — I like (it). — Echoing the verb in definite conjugation.
props.explanation: The most natural Hungarian answer echoes the verb in the appropriate conjugation: 'Szeretem.' (SEH-reh-tem) — using the definite conjugation because 'a magyar konyhát' is a specific, definite object. While 'Igen' alone works, the verb-echo is more natural in everyday Hungarian. It's complete, specific, and elegantly concise!

children.children: 🎉 Great news! Hungarian adjectives DON'T agree with nouns in number, gender, or case. The adjective stays exactly the same no matter what happens to the noun. This is WAY simpler than French, Spanish, or German!

In many European languages, adjectives change their form to match the noun — masculine/feminine, singular/plural, nominative/accusative... Hungarian throws all of that out the window. The adjective is like a stubborn rock: it never changes when placed before a noun.
🏠 nagy ház (NODJ HAHZ) — big house
🏘️ nagy házak (NODJ HAH-zok) — big houses — the adjective 'nagy' stays the SAME!
🏠 nagy házat (NODJ HAH-zot) — big house (accusative) — STILL 'nagy'!
🏠 nagy házban (NODJ HAHZ-bon) — in a big house — yep, STILL 'nagy'!
Compare this to French: 'grand' → 'grande' → 'grands' → 'grandes' (four forms!). In Hungarian, 'nagy' (NODJ) is always just 'nagy'. Period.
Attributive adjectives (describing a noun directly) come BEFORE the noun, just like in English. This is one place where Hungarian word order feels familiar!
🚗 piros autó (PEE-rosh OW-toh) — red car
👩 szép lány (SAYP LAANY) — beautiful girl
🐕 kis kutya (KEESH KOO-tyah) — small dog
📖 jó könyv (YOH KŌNYV) — good book
But predicative adjectives (after 'is/are') follow the noun — and here's the twist: Hungarian drops the copula ('is') entirely in 3rd person present tense!
🚗 Az autó piros. (OZ OW-toh PEE-rosh) — The car [is] red. — No 'is' needed!
🏠 A ház nagy. (OH HAHZ NODJ) — The house [is] big.
☕ A kávé meleg. (OH KAH-vay MEH-leg) — The coffee [is] warm.
⚠️ But 1st/2nd person DOES use a copula: 'Szép vagyok.' (SAYP VOD-yok) — I am beautiful.
Now let's learn the colors! These are some of the most useful adjectives for everyday life.

🍊 'Narancssárga' (NO-ronch-shahr-gah) literally means 'orange-yellow' — from 'narancs' (orange fruit) + 'sárga' (yellow). Hungarian builds color words logically! Similarly, 'rózsaszín' (ROH-zhah-seen) means 'rose-colored' — from 'rózsa' (rose) + 'szín' (color).

⚠️ 'kis' (KEESH) is used ONLY before nouns: 'kis kutya' (small dog). The standalone/predicative form is 'kicsi' (KEE-chee): 'A kutya kicsi.' (The dog is small.) Similarly, 'régi' (RAY-gee) is for things — for people's age, use 'öreg' (Ō-reg) = old or 'fiatal' (FEE-oh-tol) = young.
Hungarian comparatives and superlatives follow a beautifully regular pattern. Once you learn it, you can compare almost anything!
Step 1 — BASE FORM: Start with the adjective. Example: 'nagy' (NODJ) = big
Step 2 — COMPARATIVE: Add -bb (or a linking vowel + bb: -abb, -ebb, -obb). Example: 'nagyobb' (NOD-yobb) = bigger
Step 3 — SUPERLATIVE: Add the prefix 'leg-' to the comparative. Example: 'legnagyobb' (LEG-nod-yobb) = biggest
szép (SAYP) → szebb (SEBB) → legszebb (LEG-sebb) — beautiful → more beautiful → most beautiful
gyors (DYORSH) → gyorsabb (DYOR-shobb) → leggyorsabb (LEG-dyor-shobb) — fast → faster → fastest
drága (DRAH-gah) → drágább (DRAH-gaabb) → legdrágább (LEG-drah-gaabb) — expensive → more expensive → most expensive
hideg (HEE-deg) → hidegebb (HEE-deg-ebb) → leghidegebb (LEG-hee-deg-ebb) — cold → colder → coldest

⚠️ Irregular comparatives — memorize these!
• jó (YOH) → jobb (YOBB) → legjobb (LEG-yobb) — good → better → best
• sok (SHOK) → több (TŌBB) → legtöbb (LEG-tōbb) — many/much → more → most
• kicsi (KEE-chee) → kisebb (KEE-shebb) → legkisebb (LEG-kee-shebb) — small → smaller → smallest
Ez a ház nagyobb. (EZ OH HAHZ NOD-yobb) — This house is bigger.
Budapest a legnagyobb magyar város. (BOO-dah-pesht OH LEG-nod-yobb MOD-yar VAH-rohsh) — Budapest is the biggest Hungarian city.
A kávé jobb, mint a tea. (OH KAH-vay YOBB, MEENT OH TEH-ah) — Coffee is better than tea. ('mint' = than)
Hungarian has a wonderfully productive suffix: -i (EE). Stick it on a noun or place name, and you instantly get an adjective meaning 'of/from/related to' that thing. It's incredibly useful!
Budapest → budapesti (BOO-dah-pesh-tee) — from/of Budapest: 'budapesti étterem' = a Budapest restaurant
Amerika → amerikai (OH-meh-ree-kah-ee) — American: 'amerikai autó' = an American car
Európa → európai (EH-oo-roh-pah-ee) — European: 'európai kultúra' = European culture
nyár (NYAHR) = summer → nyári (NYAH-ree) = summery/summer: 'nyári ruha' = summer dress
tél (TAYL) = winter → téli (TAY-lee) = wintry/winter: 'téli kabát' = winter coat
hét (HAYT) = week → heti (HEH-tee) = weekly: 'heti program' = weekly program
ott (OHT) = there → ottani (OHT-toh-nee) = from there / of that place

💡 The -i suffix is one of Hungarian's most productive tools. You'll hear it everywhere: 'bécsi' (BAY-chee) = Viennese, 'házi' (HAH-zee) = homemade (from 'ház' = house), 'reggeli' (REG-geh-lee) = morning/breakfast (from 'reggel' = morning). Once you spot it, you can't unsee it!

Hungarian adjectives are like LEGO bricks: the adjective block stays exactly the same shape no matter what noun block you attach it to. A red 2×4 brick is the same whether it's on a small base plate or a giant castle. No gender agreement, no number agreement, no case agreement — the adjective brick never changes shape. In French or German, it's like having bricks that magically reshape themselves depending on what they're connected to. Hungarian keeps it simple: one shape, always.

props.title: Describe It!
props.prompt: Build the correct Hungarian phrases! Combine the adjective with the noun, or form the comparative.

challenges.hint: Comparative of szép is 'szebb', then add 'leg-' prefix

challenges.prompt: Say 'The house is big.' in Hungarian: A ház _____.
challenges.hint: Predicative adjective — no copula needed in 3rd person present!

challenges.prompt: Make an adjective from 'Budapest' using the -i suffix: _____
challenges.hint: Just add -i to the place name

props.question: What color is 'kék' (KAYK)?
props.explanation: 'Kék' (KAYK) means blue! Remember: piros (PEE-rosh) = red, zöld (ZŌLD) = green, sárga (SHAHR-gah) = yellow.

props.question: What is the comparative form of 'jó' (YOH) = good?
props.explanation: 'Jó' (good) has an IRREGULAR comparative: 'jobb' (YOBB) = better. The superlative is 'legjobb' (LEG-yobb) = best. This is one of the few irregulars you need to memorize!

props.question: How do you say 'The car is red' in Hungarian?
options: 'Piros az autó van.' (PEE-rosh OZ OW-toh VON)
'Az autó van piros.' (OZ OW-toh VON PEE-rosh)
props.explanation: 'Az autó piros.' (OZ OW-toh PEE-rosh) — The car [is] red. In Hungarian, 3rd person present tense doesn't need a copula ('is/are'). The subject comes first with its article, then the predicate adjective follows. No 'van' needed!

children.children: Time to learn the calendar and the clock! Hungarian has its own unique way of telling time that works BACKWARDS compared to English. Let's start with the days and months, then tackle the clock.

props.title: Days of the Week — A hét napjai (AH HAYT NOP-yai)

children.children: Days of the week are NOT capitalized in Hungarian! Write 'hétfő' not 'Hétfő' (unless it starts a sentence). To say 'on Monday,' add -n/-on/-en/-ön to the day: 'hétfőn' (HAYT-főn) = on Monday, 'kedden' (KED-den) = on Tuesday, 'szerdán' (SER-dahn) = on Wednesday.

props.title: Months of the Year — Az év hónapjai (OZ AYV HOH-nop-yai)

children.children: Just like days, months are NOT capitalized in Hungarian! To say 'in January,' use the suffix -ban/-ben: 'januárban' (YAH-noo-ahr-bon) = in January, 'februárban' (FEB-roo-ahr-bon) = in February, 'márciusban' (MAHR-tsee-oosh-bon) = in March. Back-vowel months get -ban, front-vowel months get -ben: 'szeptemberben' (SEP-tem-ber-ben).
Now for the fun part — telling time in Hungarian. Buckle up, because it works completely differently from English!

props.analogy: Imagine you're always looking AHEAD to the next hour, not back at the current one. In English, 2:30 = 'half past two' (looking back). In Hungarian, 2:30 = 'fél három' = 'half [to] three' (looking forward!). It's like Hungarian speakers are always eager to get to the next hour.

props.title: How to Tell Time in Hungarian
steps: 'Hány óra?' (HAHN-y OH-rah?) = 'What time is it?' — This is the question.
Full hours: [number] + 'óra' (OH-rah). 'Két óra' (KAYT OH-rah) = 2 o'clock. 'Öt óra' (ŌT OH-rah) = 5 o'clock.
Quarter past (X:15): 'negyed' (NE-dyed) + NEXT hour. 'Negyed három' (NE-dyed HAH-rom) = 2:15 (a quarter of the way to 3!).
Half past (X:30): 'fél' (FAYL) + NEXT hour. 'Fél három' (FAYL HAH-rom) = 2:30 (halfway to 3!).
Three-quarter (X:45): 'háromnegyed' (HAH-rom-NE-dyed) + NEXT hour. 'Háromnegyed három' (HAH-rom-NE-dyed HAH-rom) = 2:45 (three-quarters of the way to 3!).

children.children: 🧠 This is the OPPOSITE of English! In Hungarian, you always reference the NEXT hour. 'Fél három' does NOT mean 'half past three' (3:30) — it means 'half to three' (2:30). This trips up EVERY English speaker!

props.output: 'Negyed négy.' (NE-dyed NAYJ) — A quarter of the way to four.
props.explanation: For 3:15, we think about the NEXT hour (4 = négy). 'Negyed' means 'quarter.' So 'negyed négy' = a quarter [of the way to] four = 3:15.
For 7:30, the next hour is 8 (nyolc). 'Fél' means 'half.' So 'fél nyolc' = half [to] eight = 7:30. NOT 8:30!
For 11:45, the next hour is 12 (tizenkettő). 'Háromnegyed' means 'three-quarters.' So 'háromnegyed tizenkettő' = three-quarters [to] twelve = 11:45.

props.title: What Time Is It? — Hány óra?

challenges.prompt: How do you say 5:00 in Hungarian?

challenges.hint: Remember: 'fél' + next hour. Hat = 6, so half to 6 = ?

challenges.prompt: How do you say 8:15 in Hungarian?
challenges.hint: Quarter to the NEXT hour. After 8 comes 9 (kilenc).
'Háromnegyed' = three-quarters. Tíz = 10. Three-quarters of the way to 10 = ?

challenges.prompt: How do you say 'on Wednesday' in Hungarian?
challenges.hint: Szerda + the locative suffix for 'on a day.'

challenges.prompt: How do you say 'in March' in Hungarian?

props.question: Which of these is the correct Hungarian for 'Thursday'?
props.explanation: 'Csütörtök' (CHŪ-tōr-tōk) = Thursday. 'Péntek' = Friday, 'szerda' = Wednesday, 'kedd' = Tuesday. Remember: days are NOT capitalized in Hungarian!

props.question: What time does 'fél öt' (FAYL ŌT) represent?
props.explanation: 'Fél öt' = half to five = 4:30. Remember, Hungarian time looks FORWARD to the next hour! 'Fél' (half) + 'öt' (five) = halfway to 5 = 4:30. This is NOT 5:30!

props.title: 15. Verbal Prefixes — The Words That Come and Go

children.children: Hungarian has small words called verbal prefixes (igekötők, EE-geh-kö-tők) that attach to the FRONT of verbs to change or refine their meaning. They work like English 'up' in 'eat up,' 'out' in 'find out,' or 'over' in 'look over' — but with a twist: they can DETACH from the verb and jump around the sentence depending on word order, focus, and negation!

Verbal prefixes are one of Hungarian's most distinctive features. In a neutral sentence, the prefix sits glued to the front of the verb: 'megeszi' (MEG-eh-see) = 'eats it up.' But the moment you add focus, negation, or a question word, the prefix SPLITS OFF and hops behind the verb: 'NEM eszi meg' (NEM EH-see MEG) = 'does NOT eat it up.' This prefix dance is the heartbeat of Hungarian sentence structure.

items.definition: A small word that attaches to the front of a verb to add meaning — direction, completion, beginning, or change of meaning. In neutral sentences it's joined to the verb; under focus/negation it separates.
The default position in neutral, affirmative statements. The prefix is written as one word with the verb: 'megeszem' (MEG-eh-sem) = I eat it up.
When something takes focus position before the verb, the prefix splits off and moves AFTER the verb: 'NEM eszem meg' (NEM EH-sem MEG) = I don't eat it up.

children.children: Let's meet the most common verbal prefixes. Each one carries a core meaning, but many have extended or idiomatic uses too.

The most important prefix is 'meg-' (MEG). It usually signals that an action is COMPLETED — think of it as the difference between 'I was eating' (eszem) and 'I ate it all up' (megettem, MEG-et-tem). This completed/perfective meaning is crucial: 'Írok egy levelet' = 'I'm writing a letter' (ongoing) vs 'Megírok egy levelet' = 'I'll write a letter (and finish it)' (completed).
Watch how prefixes transform the simple verb 'megy' (MEDJ) = 'goes':
• elmegy (EL-medj) — goes away, leaves
• kimegy (KEE-medj) — goes out
• bemegy (BEH-medj) — goes in
• felmegy (FEL-medj) — goes up
• lemegy (LEH-medj) — goes down
• átmegy (AAHT-medj) — goes across
• visszamegy (VIS-sah-medj) — goes back
• odamegy (OH-dah-medj) — goes over there
That's EIGHT different verbs from one root, each with a clear and logical directional meaning! And every single one follows the same prefix-attachment and prefix-detachment rules.

props.explanation: Think of the prefix as a small booster rocket strapped to the nose of a spaceship (the verb). In normal flight (neutral sentences), the booster stays attached at the front: 'meg-eszik' — full power, cruising along. But when something demands the spotlight (focus, negation, a question word), that spotlight-seeker shoves its way in front of the verb, and the booster rocket DETACHES and drifts behind: 'NEM eszik meg.' The booster hasn't disappeared — it's just floating behind the ship, waiting. In the next neutral sentence, it reattaches to the front.

children.children: Now let's see the prefix dance in action. This is where Sections 10 (Word Order) and 12 (Questions) come alive!

rows: Péter NEM eszi meg az almát.
PAY-ter NEM EH-see MEG oz OL-maat
Peter does NOT eat up the apple.
It's PETER who eats up the apple.
It's THE APPLE that Peter eats up.

children.children: ⚡ THE RULE: The prefix stays attached ONLY in neutral, affirmative statements with no special focus. The moment ANYTHING takes the focus position before the verb — negation (nem), a question word (ki, mit, hol...), or an emphasized element — the prefix DETACHES and moves after the verb. This is not optional — it's a hard grammatical rule!

items.title: Step 1: Is the sentence neutral and affirmative?
items.description: If YES → prefix stays ATTACHED to the front of the verb: 'Megeszem.' (MEG-eh-sem) = 'I eat it up.' Move to done!

items.title: Step 2: Is there negation (nem)?
items.description: If YES → 'nem' goes before the verb, prefix DETACHES and moves after: 'NEM eszem meg.' (NEM EH-sem MEG) = 'I don't eat it up.'

items.title: Step 3: Is there a focused element or question word?
items.description: If YES → the focused word/question word goes before the verb, prefix DETACHES: 'KI eszi meg?' (KEE EH-see MEG?) = 'WHO eats it up?'

items.title: Step 4: Check — is the prefix behind the verb?
items.description: If the prefix detached, it should appear immediately AFTER the conjugated verb. It doesn't float to the end of the sentence — it stays close: 'nem eszem MEG az almát' ✓ (not *'nem eszem az almát meg').

children.children: Many prefix + verb combinations don't just add direction — they create entirely NEW meanings, just like English phrasal verbs ('give up' ≠ 'give' + 'up'). Here are some common ones:

rows: looks out / appears (as in looks good/bad)

children.children: Notice how 'ír' (writes) becomes FIVE completely different verbs depending on the prefix! This is extremely productive — learning a new prefix instantly unlocks dozens of new verb meanings.

Hungarian doesn't have a simple past/present/future tense system like English. Instead, it relies heavily on ASPECT — whether an action is completed or ongoing. The prefix 'meg-' (and others) often marks an action as COMPLETED (perfective), while the bare verb without a prefix often implies an ongoing or habitual action (imperfective).
Compare these pairs:
• 'Eszem az almát.' (EH-sem oz OL-maat) — 'I'm eating the apple.' (ongoing, in progress)
• 'Megeszem az almát.' (MEG-eh-sem oz OL-maat) — 'I'll eat up the apple.' / 'I eat the apple (completely).' (completed action)
• 'Írom a levelet.' (EE-rom ah LEH-veh-let) — 'I'm writing the letter.' (in progress)
• 'Megírom a levelet.' (MEG-ee-rom ah LEH-veh-let) — 'I'll write the letter (and finish it).' (will be completed)

In the present tense, adding 'meg-' often shifts the meaning to future — because if the action is being framed as completed, and you're saying it now, it must be about to happen! This is how Hungarian expresses future tense for specific actions without a dedicated 'will' word.
A: 'Megírtad a levelet?' (MEG-eer-tod ah LEH-veh-let?) — 'Did you write (finish) the letter?'
B: 'Nem, NEM írtam meg.' (NEM, NEM EER-tom MEG.) — 'No, I did NOT write it.' — Negation forces prefix behind verb!
A: 'Ki írja meg?' (KEE EER-yah MEG?) — 'WHO will write it?' — Question word forces prefix behind verb!
B: 'MARI írja meg.' (MO-ree EER-yah MEG.) — 'MARI will write it.' — Focus on Mari forces prefix behind verb!
A: 'Jó, akkor Mari megírja.' (YOH, OK-kor MO-ree MEG-eer-yah.) — 'OK, then Mari will write it.' — Neutral statement, prefix reattaches!
Notice the prefix 'meg' dancing back and forth: attached in neutral statements, detached under negation/focus/questions, then reattached when neutral again. This pattern is 100% consistent!

props.title: Attach or Detach? — Prefix Position Practice
props.description: For each sentence, decide: should the verbal prefix be ATTACHED (before the verb) or DETACHED (after the verb)? Think about whether the sentence is neutral, negated, or has a focused element.

children.children: 🧩 1. 'Péter ___eszik / eszi___ az almát.' (Peter eats up the apple — neutral statement) → Answer: ATTACHED — 'Péter megeszi az almát.' (PAY-ter MEG-eh-see oz OL-maat) — Neutral and affirmative, so the prefix stays glued to the front.

🧩 2. 'Péter NEM ___eszi / eszi___ az almát.' (Peter does NOT eat up the apple) → Answer: DETACHED — 'Péter NEM eszi meg az almát.' (PAY-ter NEM EH-see MEG oz OL-maat) — Negation takes focus, so the prefix splits off behind the verb.
🧩 3. 'KI ___eszi / eszi___ az almát?' (WHO eats up the apple?) → Answer: DETACHED — 'KI eszi meg az almát?' (KEE EH-see MEG oz OL-maat) — Question word takes focus, prefix detaches.

🧩 4. 'Holnap ___írom / írom___ a levelet.' (Tomorrow I'll write the letter — neutral, 'holnap' is just a time adverb, not focused) → Answer: ATTACHED — 'Holnap megírom a levelet.' (HOL-nop MEG-ee-rom ah LEH-veh-let) — Time adverbs in topic position don't steal focus, so the prefix stays attached.

🧩 5. 'HOLNAP ___írom / írom___ a levelet!' (It's TOMORROW that I'll write the letter — 'holnap' is focused/emphasized!) → Answer: DETACHED — 'HOLNAP írom meg a levelet!' (HOL-nop EE-rom MEG ah LEH-veh-let) — Now 'holnap' is in focus (stressed, contrastive), so the prefix detaches. Same word, different position = different prefix behavior!

🔗 CONNECTION TO PREVIOUS SECTIONS: Prefix detachment is the mechanical proof of focus! In Section 10 (Word Order), we learned that the focus position is right before the verb. In Section 12 (Questions), we saw question words must sit before the verb. Now you can see WHY the prefix moves — it was occupying the focus slot, and when something more important claims that slot, the prefix gets bumped. The prefix is like a placeholder for 'nothing special to emphasize' — when there IS something special, it steps aside.

questions.question: In the sentence 'Anna megírja a levelet' (ON-nah MEG-eer-yah ah LEH-veh-let), the prefix 'meg' is attached to the verb. What does this tell you about the sentence?
options: It's a neutral, affirmative statement with no special focus
questions.explanation: When the prefix is attached (meg + írja = megírja), the sentence is neutral and affirmative — no negation, no question word, no special focus. It simply means 'Anna will write the letter.' If anything were focused, negated, or questioned, the prefix would detach!

questions.question: How do you say 'Peter did NOT go away' using the verb 'elmegy' (EL-medj, goes away)?
questions.explanation: 'Nem' (not) takes the focus position before the verb, forcing the prefix 'el-' to detach and move behind the verb. Past tense of 'megy' is 'ment,' so: 'Péter nem ment el.' (PAY-ter NEM MENT EL) = 'Peter did not go away.' The prefix 'el' floats behind the verb 'ment.'

questions.question: What is the difference between 'Írom a levelet' and 'Megírom a levelet'?
options: No difference — they're identical in meaning
'Írom' is present tense, 'megírom' is past tense
'Írom' implies ongoing/in-progress writing; 'megírom' implies the writing will be completed
questions.explanation: 'Írom a levelet' (EE-rom ah LEH-veh-let) = 'I'm writing the letter' — ongoing, in progress. 'Megírom a levelet' (MEG-ee-rom ah LEH-veh-let) = 'I'll write (finish) the letter' — the 'meg-' prefix adds a sense of completion (perfective aspect). In present tense, this perfective meaning often shifts to future: I WILL complete it.

questions.question: In 'KI eszi meg az almát?' (Who eats up the apple?), why is 'meg' AFTER the verb?
options: Because 'meg' always comes after the verb in questions
Because the question word 'ki' claims the focus position before the verb, bumping the prefix behind it
Because the sentence is in past tense
questions.explanation: Question words are always in focus — they MUST sit immediately before the verb. Since the prefix 'meg-' normally occupies the pre-verb position, the question word 'ki' displaces it. The prefix doesn't disappear; it moves behind the verb: 'KI eszi meg...?' This is the same focus-displacement rule that applies to negation and emphasized elements.

children.children: Good news: Hungarian has only ONE past tense! No distinction between 'I ate,' 'I was eating,' 'I have eaten,' or 'I had eaten.' The single Hungarian past tense — formed with a -t/-tt suffix on the verb — covers all of these. Context and verbal prefixes (Section 15) handle the rest.

In English, you juggle multiple past forms: simple past, past continuous, present perfect, past perfect... Hungarian cuts through all of that with one elegant system: add -t or -tt to the verb stem, then add your personal ending. That's it. The same definite/indefinite conjugation split from Section 8 still applies, so you'll need both sets of endings — but the past tense marker is always the same trusty -t/-tt.

items.definition: Added to the verb stem to signal past tense. Verbs ending in a vowel get -tt (tanul → tanult). Verbs ending in certain consonants get -ott/-ett/-ött (a linking vowel + t). Some verbs just get -t directly.
Used when there's no definite object (same rules as Section 8). 'Olvastam.' (OL-vosh-tom) = 'I read (something).'
Used when the object is definite (same rules as Section 8). 'Olvastam.' (OL-vosh-tom) = 'I read it / I read THE book.' — Yes, the first person forms happen to be identical!

children.children: Let's see the full past tense conjugation using 'lát' (LAHT) = 'to see':

⚠️ Notice: the 'én' (I) form is IDENTICAL for indefinite and definite: 'láttam' in both cases! Context tells you which is meant. For all other persons, the endings differ — just like in the present tense. Also notice the double -tt- : 'lát' already ends in -t, so the past tense marker creates -tt-.
Now let's see a verb that doesn't end in -t. Here's 'olvas' (OL-vosh) = 'to read':

props.title: Building the Past Tense Step by Step

children.children: Let's break down how to form the past tense of 'ír' (EER) = 'to write':
1. Start with the verb stem: ír
2. Add the past tense marker -t: írt-
3. Add the personal ending:
• írtam (EER-tom) — I wrote
• írtál (EER-taal) — you wrote
• írt (EERT) — he/she wrote
• írtunk (EER-toonk) — we wrote
• írtatok (EER-tah-tok) — you (pl.) wrote
• írtak (EER-tok) — they wrote
Notice: the 3rd person singular indefinite is just stem + t with NO additional ending: 'írt' = he/she wrote. This is the shortest form!

props.title: Tricky Stems — When the Past Tense Marker Needs Help

children.children: Some verb stems need a linking vowel before the -t, because consonant clusters would be unpronounceable. The linking vowel follows vowel harmony:

⚠️ The irregular verbs 'jön/megy/eszik/iszik/van/tesz/vesz/visz' are among the most common verbs in the language. Their past tense forms MUST be memorized — no shortcut! The good news: once you know the 3rd person singular past form, the rest of the endings are completely regular.

props.explanation: English past tense is like a complex filing system with multiple drawers: 'I ate' (simple past), 'I was eating' (past continuous), 'I have eaten' (present perfect), 'I had eaten' (past perfect). Hungarian past tense is like ONE drawer with a single label: -t/-tt. Throw everything in there! 'Ettem' can mean 'I ate,' 'I was eating,' 'I have eaten,' or 'I had eaten' — context and verbal prefixes sort out the details. Fewer drawers, less confusion.

children.children: Now let's see how past tense works with verbal prefixes (Section 15) — this is where it all comes together!

rows: I did NOT write the letter.

props.title: One Past Tense, Many English Translations

children.children: The same Hungarian past tense form can translate to multiple English tenses. Context does the work:
• 'Ettem.' (ET-tem) — 'I ate.' / 'I was eating.' / 'I have eaten.'
• 'Már ettem.' (MAAR ET-tem) — 'I have already eaten.' (The word 'már' = already provides the context.)
• 'Éppen ettem, amikor...' (AYP-pen ET-tem, AH-mee-kor) — 'I was eating when...' (The word 'éppen' = just then provides continuous context.)
• 'Megettem az almát.' (MEG-et-tem oz OL-maat) — 'I ate up the apple.' / 'I finished eating the apple.' (The prefix 'meg-' signals completion.)
See how Hungarian uses context words (már, éppen) and verbal prefixes (meg-) instead of different tense forms? One past tense, infinite precision!

props.title: A Day in the Past — Mini Story

children.children: Tegnap reggel felkeltem. (TEG-nop REG-gel FEL-kel-tem) — Yesterday morning I got up.
Megittam egy kávét. (MEG-it-tom edj KAA-vayt) — I drank (finished) a coffee.
Elmentem a boltba. (EL-men-tem ah BOLT-bah) — I went to the store.
Vettem három almát. (VET-tem HAA-rom OL-maat) — I bought three apples.
Hazajöttem és főztem. (HAH-zah-yōt-tem aysh FŌZ-tem) — I came home and cooked.
Jó napom volt! (YOH NAH-pom VOLT) — I had a good day! (Literally: 'Good my-day was!')
Notice all the past tense -t/-tt markers and the verbal prefixes (fel-, meg-, el-, haza-) adding direction and completion to each action!

props.title: Put It in the Past!
props.description: Convert these present tense sentences to past tense. Remember: add -t/-tt to the verb stem, then your personal ending.

children.children: 🧩 1. Present: 'Látok egy kutyát.' (LAH-tok edj KOO-tyaht) = 'I see a dog.' → Past: 'Láttam egy kutyát.' (LAHT-tom edj KOO-tyaht) = 'I saw a dog.' (lát → látt + am)
🧩 2. Present: 'Péter olvas.' (PAY-ter OL-vosh) = 'Peter reads.' → Past: 'Péter olvasott.' (PAY-ter OL-voh-shott) = 'Peter read.' (olvas → olvas + ott)

🧩 3. Present: 'Megeszem az almát.' (MEG-eh-sem oz OL-maat) = 'I'll eat up the apple.' → Past: 'Megettem az almát.' (MEG-et-tem oz OL-maat) = 'I ate up the apple.' (eszik → evett, irregular! én form: ettem)
🧩 4. Present: 'Mari elmegy.' (MO-ree EL-medj) = 'Mari leaves.' → Past: 'Mari elment.' (MO-ree EL-ment) = 'Mari left.' (megy → ment, irregular!)
🧩 5. Present: 'Iszom egy sört.' (EE-som edj SHŌRT) = 'I drink a beer.' → Past: 'Ittam egy sört.' (IT-tom edj SHŌRT) = 'I drank a beer.' (iszik → ivott, irregular! én form: ittam)

questions.question: What is the past tense of 'van' (is)?
questions.explanation: 'Van' (is) has a completely irregular past tense: 'volt' (VOLT). This is one of the most common words in Hungarian — 'Ő itt volt.' (Ō ITT VOLT) = 'He/she was here.' Just memorize it!

questions.question: How would you say 'We saw the movie' using 'lát' (see) + 'a filmet' (the movie)?
questions.explanation: 'A filmet' (the movie) is a DEFINITE object (it has 'a' = the), so you need the definite conjugation. 'We' in definite past = 'láttuk' (LAHT-took). 'Láttuk a filmet.' = 'We saw the movie.' 'Láttunk' would be the indefinite form (used if there were no definite object).

questions.question: Why can 'ettem' mean both 'I ate' AND 'I was eating'?
options: Because Hungarian has no past tense
Because Hungarian has only ONE past tense form — context and adverbs distinguish ongoing from completed
Because 'ettem' is irregular and has multiple meanings
Because the definite and indefinite forms are the same
questions.explanation: Hungarian has a single past tense that covers all English past forms (simple, continuous, perfect, etc.). 'Ettem' = I ate / I was eating / I have eaten. Context words like 'már' (already), 'éppen' (just then), and verbal prefixes like 'meg-' (completion) disambiguate when needed.

questions.question: In 'NEM láttam meg a macskát' (I didn't see/spot the cat), why is 'meg' AFTER 'láttam'?
options: Because 'meg' always comes after the verb in past tense
Because 'nem' takes the focus position before the verb, forcing the prefix to detach
questions.explanation: Prefix detachment rules (Section 15) apply equally in past tense! 'Nem' claims the focus position before the verb, so the prefix 'meg' must detach and move behind: 'NEM láttam meg.' The same rule works in present, past, and any other context — focus always wins the pre-verb slot.

props.title: 17. Future Tense & Conditional — Will and Would

children.children: Hungarian has NO dedicated future tense conjugation! Instead, it uses the present tense with context, or the auxiliary 'fog' + infinitive for emphasis. The conditional mood (-na/-ne/-ná/-né) handles 'would' — and it's beautifully regular.

After mastering the past tense (Section 16), you might expect a complicated future tense to learn. Instead, Hungarian does something elegant: the present tense does double duty. Just like English 'I'm leaving tomorrow' uses a present form for a future event, Hungarian does this as its DEFAULT strategy.
Strategy 1 — Present tense + time word (most common, most natural):

Holnap megyek.        (HOL-nop ME-dyek)       = Tomorrow I go / I'll go.
Jövő héten utazunk.   (YÖ-vő HAY-ten OO-to-zoonk) = Next week we travel / we'll travel.
Este főzök.           (ESH-te FŐ-zök)         = Tonight I cook / I'll cook.
Strategy 2 — 'fog' + infinitive (explicit future, slightly formal or emphatic):

fogok      (FO-gok)      = I will        fogunk   (FO-goonk)  = we will
fogsz      (FOGS)        = you will      fogtok   (FOG-tok)   = you (pl.) will
fog        (FOG)         = he/she will   fognak   (FOG-nok)   = they will

Fogok főzni.           (FO-gok FŐZ-ni)        = I WILL cook.
Fog esni.              (FOG ESH-ni)            = It WILL rain.
Fogják látni.          (FOG-yahk LAHT-ni)      = They WILL see it.

Notice: 'fog' conjugates like a regular verb (indefinite), while the main verb stays in its infinitive form (-ni). When the object is definite, 'fog' takes definite endings: fogom, fogod, fogja, fogjuk, fogjátok, fogják.
Strategy 3 — Verbal prefix (especially 'meg-') with present tense implies completion/future:

Megcsinálom.           (MEG-chi-NAH-lom)       = I'll do it / I'll get it done.
Megesszük.             (MEG-es-sük)            = We'll eat it (all of it).

Think of how English handles the future: 'I will go,' 'I'm going to go,' 'I go tomorrow,' 'I'm going tomorrow' — four different constructions, none of which is a true verb conjugation. English doesn't really have a future 'tense' either — 'will' is a modal verb, not an ending!

Hungarian just made this explicit: don't pretend there's a future tense. Use the present and let context do the work. The 'fog' construction exists for when you really need to emphasize futurity, like saying 'I WILL do it' vs 'I'll do it.'
Now for something Hungarian DOES conjugate: the conditional mood.

The conditional expresses 'would,' hypotheticals, polite requests, and wishes. It's formed by adding -na/-ne (or -ná/-né for certain persons) to the verb stem, followed by personal endings. Vowel harmony determines whether you use -na (back vowel) or -ne (front vowel).

INDEFINITE CONDITIONAL — lát (LAHT) = to see (back vowel → -na)

látnék     (LAHT-nayk)    = I would see
látnál     (LAHT-nahl)    = you would see
látna      (LAHT-no)      = he/she would see
látnánk    (LAHT-nahnk)   = we would see
látnátok   (LAHT-nah-tok) = you (pl.) would see
látnának   (LAHT-nah-nok) = they would see

DEFINITE CONDITIONAL — lát (LAHT) = to see

látnám     (LAHT-nahm)    = I would see it
látnád     (LAHT-nahd)    = you would see it
látná      (LAHT-nah)     = he/she would see it
látnánk    (LAHT-nahnk)   = we would see it  ← same as indefinite!
látnátok   (LAHT-nah-tok) = you (pl.) would see it  ← same!
látnák     (LAHT-nahk)    = they would see it
Now with a front-vowel verb — kér (KAYR) = to ask/request:

INDEFINITE:  kérnék, kérnél, kérne, kérnénk, kérnétek, kérnének
             (KAYR-nayk, KAYR-nayl, KAYR-ne, KAYR-naynk, KAYR-nay-tek, KAYR-nay-nek)

DEFINITE:    kérném, kérnéd, kérné, kérnénk, kérnétek, kérnék
             (KAYR-naym, KAYR-nayd, KAYR-nay, KAYR-naynk, KAYR-nay-tek, KAYR-nayk)
Polite requests (the most common use!):

Kérnék egy kávét.      (KAYR-nayk edj KAH-vayt)     = I'd like a coffee.
Megnéznéd?             (MEG-nayz-nayd)               = Would you look at it?
Elmondaná?             (EL-mon-do-nah)               = Would he/she tell (it)?
Segítenél?             (SHE-gee-te-nayl)             = Would you help?
Hypotheticals and wishes:

Ha lenne időm, olvasnék.        = If I had time, I would read.
(HO LEN-ne I-dőm, OL-vosh-nayk)

Szívesen mennék Budapestre.     = I'd gladly go to Budapest.
(SEE-ve-shen MEN-nayk BOO-do-pesht-re)

Bárcsak tudnám!                 = If only I knew (it)!
(BAHR-chok TOOD-nahm)

Watch out for 'ha' (if) clauses! Unlike English, Hungarian uses the conditional in BOTH halves of 'if...would' sentences: 'Ha lenne időm, olvasnék' — literally 'If I would-have time, I would-read.' English only uses 'would' in the result clause.

holnap        (HOL-nop)         = tomorrow
holnapután    (HOL-nop-OO-tahn) = day after tomorrow
jövőre        (YÖ-vő-re)       = next year
jövő héten    (YÖ-vő HAY-ten)  = next week
később        (KAY-shőbb)      = later
hamarosan     (HO-mo-ro-shon)  = soon
mindjárt      (MIND-yahrt)     = right away / in a moment
ma este       (MO ESH-te)      = tonight (literally: today evening)
egyszer       (EDJ-ser)        = someday / once
Compare these three sentences — same verb (megy = to go), three different moods:

PAST:         Elmentem a boltba.     = I went to the store.
              (EL-men-tem o BOLT-bo)

FUTURE:       Holnap elmegyek.       = Tomorrow I'll go.
              (HOL-nop EL-me-dyek)

CONDITIONAL:  Elmennék, de nincs idő. = I'd go, but there's no time.
              (EL-men-nayk, de NINCH I-dő)

Notice how the verbal prefix 'el-' (away) stays attached in all three. The stem changes: ment- (past), megy- (present/future), menne- (conditional). Irregular? A little — but 'megy' (to go) is one of Hungarian's handful of truly irregular verbs.

questions.question: How do you say 'I'll cook tomorrow' most naturally in Hungarian?
