questions.explanation: 'Holnap főzök' — present tense + time word is the most natural way. 'Fogok főzni holnap' is grammatically correct but sounds overly emphatic. 'Főznék' is conditional ('I would cook'), and 'főztem' is past tense.

questions.question: What does 'Kérnék egy sört' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Kérnék' is first person singular conditional of 'kér' (to request) — 'I would request/like.' 'Egy sört' = 'a beer' (accusative). This is the standard polite way to order something in Hungarian.

questions.question: In a Hungarian 'if...would' sentence, where does the conditional mood appear?
options: Only in the result clause (like English)
In neither clause — Hungarian uses subjunctive instead
questions.explanation: Unlike English, Hungarian uses the conditional in both halves: 'Ha lenne időm, olvasnék' (If I would-have time, I would-read). English only puts 'would' in the result clause.

questions.question: What is the definite conditional form of 'lát' (to see) for 'they would see it'?
questions.explanation: 'Látnák' (LAHT-nahk) is third person plural definite conditional. 'Látnának' is the indefinite form ('they would see something'). 'Látná' is third person singular definite ('he/she would see it'). 'Látnám' is first person singular definite ('I would see it').

children.children: The imperative is Hungarian's third mood (after indicative and conditional). It uses the suffix -j- as its signature marker, but this -j- interacts with the verb stem in wonderfully unpredictable ways. The imperative also doubles as the subjunctive — 'that I may go' uses the same form as 'go!'

You already know the indicative (plain facts: 'I go') and the conditional ('I would go'). The imperative handles everything from sharp commands ('Go!') to polite requests ('Please sit down') to first-person intentions ('Let me think') to purpose clauses ('so that we can leave'). It's one of the most-used moods in everyday Hungarian.

Think of the Hungarian imperative as a Swiss Army knife. In English, you need separate constructions for 'Go!' (command), 'Let's go!' (suggestion), 'Let him go' (permission), and 'so that he goes' (purpose). Hungarian uses ONE mood for all of these — just change the person suffix. The -j- marker is the handle; the personal endings are the different blades.
The basic recipe: verb stem + -j- + personal ending. For indefinite conjugation:

várni (to wait) → stem: vár-

várjak      (VAHR-yok)       = let me wait / that I wait
várj        (VAHRJ)          = wait! (you, informal)
várjon      (VAHR-yon)       = wait! (you, formal)
várjunk     (VAHR-yoonk)     = let's wait
várjatok    (VAHR-yo-tok)    = wait! (you all)
várjanak    (VAHR-yo-nok)    = let them wait
For definite conjugation (when there's a specific object — see Section 8):

várni (to wait for [it]) → stem: vár-

várjam      (VAHR-yom)       = let me wait for it
várjad/várd (VAHR-yod/VAHRD) = wait for it! (informal)
várja       (VAHR-yo)        = wait for it! (formal)
várjuk      (VAHR-yook)      = let's wait for it
várjátok    (VAHR-YAH-tok)   = wait for it! (you all)
várják      (VAHR-yahk)      = let them wait for it
Notice that the 2nd person singular informal has TWO forms: várjad (full) and várd (short). The short form is much more common in everyday speech. 'Várd meg!' = 'Wait for it!'

Here's where it gets interesting. The -j- doesn't always stay as 'j'. When it meets certain consonants at the end of the verb stem, it transforms — sometimes into a double consonant, sometimes disappearing entirely into a new sound. This is the imperative's signature challenge.

Stems ending in -s, -sz, -z, -dz:
  -j- ASSIMILATES into the consonant (doubles it)

  olvas + j → olvass!     (OL-vosh)      read!
  néz + j   → nézz!       (NAYZ)         look!
  hoz + j   → hozz!       (HOZ)          bring!
  játszik + j → játssz!   (YAHT-s)       play!

Stems ending in -t:
  Most → -ts- (written -ss-)
  lát + j    → láss!      (LAHSH)        see!
  tanít + j  → taníts!    (TO-neetch)    teach!
  BUT some -t stems keep the -j-:
  tart + j   → tartson!   (TORT-shon)    hold! (formal)
  fut + j    → fusson!    (FOOSH-shon)   run! (formal)

The -t stems are the trickiest. There's no perfect rule — you learn them with practice. But the most common pattern: if the -t is preceded by a vowel or by -s, -sz, the -j- assimilates. If preceded by another consonant, it often becomes -s- before the ending.

Gyere!        (DYE-re)           Come! (informal, irregular)
Gyerünk!      (DYE-rünk)         Let's go! (informal, very common)
Menj!         (MENY)             Go! (informal)
Menjen!       (MEN-yen)          Go! (formal)
Ülj le!       (ÜLY le)           Sit down! (informal)
Állj meg!     (AHLY meg)         Stop! (informal)
Nézd!         (NAYZD)            Look at it! (informal, definite)
Figyelj!      (FI-dyely)         Pay attention! (informal)
Várj!         (VAHRJ)            Wait! (informal)
Hagyd abba!   (HODYD OB-bo)      Stop it! / Leave it!

Note 'gyere' — this is completely irregular. The standard imperative of 'jön' (to come) would be 'jöjj', but in everyday speech, 'gyere' has almost entirely replaced it. Similarly, 'gyerünk!' ('let's go!') is far more natural than the technically correct 'menjünk!'
A bare imperative can sound harsh. Hungarian has several ways to soften it:

1. Use formal (3rd person) instead of informal (2nd person):
   Ülj le!    → Üljön le!     (formal 'sit down')
   Gyere!     → Jöjjön!       (formal 'come')

2. Add 'kérem' (I request) or 'légy szíves' (be kind):
   Légy szíves, segíts!       (LAYDY SEE-vesh, SHE-geets)
   = Be so kind, help!  (informal)

   Kérem, üljön le.           (KAY-rem, ÜL-yön le)
   = Please, sit down.  (formal)

3. Add '-szíveskedjen' for very formal/bureaucratic:
   Szíveskedjen aláírni.      (SEE-vesh-KED-yen O-lah-eer-ni)
   = Would you be so kind as to sign. (officialese)

The imperative form doubles as the subjunctive in Hungarian — used in purpose clauses ('so that...'), wishes, and after certain verbs. This is why Hungarians sometimes say 'that I go' when they mean 'let me go' — it's the same form!

Purpose clause ('so that'):
  Sietek, hogy elérjem a buszt.
  (SHE-e-tek, hody EL-ayr-yem o BOOST)
  = I'm hurrying so that I catch the bus.

After 'want' (akar):
  Azt akarom, hogy menjünk.
  (OST O-ko-rom, hody MEN-yünk)
  = I want us to go. (lit: 'I want that we-go')

After 'must/should' (kell):
  Meg kell, hogy mondjam.
  (MEG kell, hody MOND-yom)
  = I must say it. (lit: 'it's needed that I-say-it')
The word 'hogy' (that) is your signal: almost any verb after 'hogy' takes the imperative/subjunctive form. This is one of the most common patterns in spoken Hungarian.
Important: negative commands use 'ne' (not 'nem'!). This is one of the few places where negation changes form:

Statement:  Nem megyek.     = I don't go.     (indicative → nem)
Command:    Ne menj!        = Don't go!       (imperative → ne)

Ne félj!        (NE fayly)          Don't be afraid!
Ne aggódj!      (NE OG-gohdj)       Don't worry!
Ne csináld!     (NE CHI-nahld)      Don't do it!
Ne haragudj!    (NE HO-ro-goody)    Don't be angry! (= sorry!)
Ne zavarj!      (NE ZO-vorj)        Don't bother me!
'Ne haragudj!' is one of the most useful phrases in Hungarian — it literally means 'don't be angry' but functions as 'excuse me' or 'sorry' in everyday conversation.

props.title: All Three Moods at a Glance
rows: let me go / that I go

props.question: How do you say 'Wait!' (informal) in Hungarian?
props.explanation: Várj! uses the -j imperative suffix for informal 2nd person singular. 'Várjon!' would be the formal version.

props.question: What happens to the -j- suffix when added to 'olvas' (read)?
props.explanation: When the stem ends in -s, -sz, -z, or -dz, the -j- assimilates into the consonant, doubling it: olvas + j → olvass!

props.question: How do you negate a command in Hungarian?
props.explanation: Negative commands use 'ne', not 'nem'. 'Ne menj!' = Don't go! This is one of the few places where the negation word changes.

props.question: What does 'Azt akarom, hogy menjünk' mean?
props.explanation: 'Menjünk' after 'hogy' is the subjunctive (same form as imperative). 'Azt akarom, hogy menjünk' = 'I want that we go' = 'I want us to go.'

props.question: 'Ne haragudj!' literally means 'Don't be angry!' — but what does it actually function as?
props.explanation: 'Ne haragudj!' is one of the most common phrases in Hungarian, used as 'sorry' or 'excuse me' in everyday conversation — not as a literal plea about anger.
